title: PCOS
oneLiner: A common endocrine disorder of reproductive-age women, characterised by hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, and polycystic ovaries; it increases long-term metabolic and cardiovascular risk.

sections.Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS): **One-liner:** A common endocrine disorder of reproductive-age women, characterised by hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, and polycystic ovaries; it increases long-term metabolic and cardiovascular risk.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ovarian torsion** (rare but surgical emergency): sudden, severe unilateral pelvic pain with nausea/vomiting; absent arterial flow on pelvic ultrasound is the killer finding – do not wait for lab results.
- **Endometrial adenocarcinoma** (driven by unopposed oestrogen in anovulatory cycles): postmenopausal bleeding or atypical endometrial cells on biopsy – not a first-hour emergency, but must not be missed as a chronic complication.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Oligomenorrhoea/amenorrhoea (<9 menses/year)
  - Hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey score ≥8)
  - Acne, androgenetic alopecia
  - Infertility
  - Weight gain/central obesity (BMI >30 in ~50%)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Acanthosis nigricans (insulin resistance)
  - Palpable ovarian masses? (unlikely, but consider US)
  - Blood pressure (elevated in metabolic syndrome)
  - Waist circumference >80 cm
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators**
  1. PCOS (most common) – chronic anovulation + hyperandrogenism + PCOM on US
  2. Non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (NCCAH) – elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone (>30 nmol/L)
  3. Cushing’s syndrome – features of cortisol excess (moon face, striae, proximal myopathy)
  4. Androgen-secreting tumour – rapid onset, severe virilisation, testosterone >5 nmol/L
  5. Hypothyroidism – elevated TSH, galactorrhoea (if prolactin elevated)
- **Splitter:** **Testosterone >5 nmol/L** – suggests androgen-secreting tumour (not PCOS). **17-OHP >30 nmol/L** – suggests NCCAH.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine pregnancy test (always in reproductive-age women with amenorrhoea)
  - Blood glucose (finger-prick if symptomatic)
- **Bloods:**
  - **Hormones:** total testosterone, SHBG, free androgen index (FAI), LH, FSH, prolactin, 17-OHP (cycle day 2-5 if possible, or random)
  - **Metabolic:** fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipid profile (HDL, LDL, triglycerides)
  - **Other:** TSH, serum cortisol (if Cushing’s suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transvaginal pelvic ultrasound (TVUS) – count antral follicles (≥20 in one ovary) or ovarian volume ≥10 mL
  - **Second-line:** Adrenal CT if tumour suspected (testosterone >5 nmol/L)
- **Special tests:**
  - Glucose tolerance test (75 g OGTT) if BMI >30 or metabolic syndrome
  - Endometrial biopsy if postmenopausal bleeding or atypical bleeding >6 months
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Rotterdam criteria (2003)** – requires ≥2 of:
  - Oligo‑ or anovulation
  - Clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism
  - Polycystic ovaries on US (≥20 follicles per ovary or volume ≥10 mL)
  - Exclude other causes (NCCAH, Cushing’s, tumour, thyroid)
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** – a woman with amenorrhoea could be pregnant, not PCOS. Also, **endometrial biopsy** in any PCOS patient with persistent intermenstrual bleeding or age >35 (risk of endometrial cancer).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute pelvic pain + suspected torsion: urgent gynaecology referral + ultrasound
  - If bleeding + anaemia: stabilise, arrange TVUS, consider tranexamic acid (if no VTE risk)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Not usually admitted for PCOS alone. If admitted for another reason (e.g. DKA, endometrial biopsy):
    - Check HbA1c, lipids, BP
    - Start lifestyle advice (diet, exercise, weight loss)
    - Refer to dietician
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **First-line:** Lifestyle modification (weight loss 5-10% improves ovulation)
  - **Pharmacotherapy:**
    - Combined oral contraceptive (COCP) for cycle control + anti-androgen effect (e.g. Yasmin® [drospirenone+ethinylestradiol])
    - Metformin: 500 mg TDS or 850-1000 mg BD (off-label but used for metabolic benefit)
    - Anti-androgens: spironolactone (100-200 mg/day) – only with COCP (contraception required, teratogenic)
    - Infertility: clomifene citrate (50-150 mg on days 2-6) – **IAN** endocrine/specialist supervision
  - **Endometrial protection:** Progesterone 10 mg for 14 days per month if unopposed oestrogen
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Not specific for PCOS; use standard hospital policy for surgery/observation
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **COCP:** generic ethinylestradiol 30 µg + levonorgestrel 150 µg daily for 21 days, 7-day break
  - **Metformin:** start 500 mg daily, increase to 500 mg TDS or 850-1000 mg BD (max 2000 mg/day)
  - **Spironolactone:** 100-200 mg daily (only with COCP)
  - **Clomifene citrate:** 50 mg daily ×5 days (cycle days 2-6); max 150 mg daily if anovulatory
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Cyproterone acetate (anti-androgen) – 25-100 mg daily ×10 days/month (use as last-line due to hepatotoxicity)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - COCP – contraindicated in BMI >35 (VTE risk), migraine with aura, uncontrolled hypertension, smoking >35 years
  - Metformin – hold if eGFR <30, risk of lactic acidosis (rare)
  - Clomifene – risk of multiple gestation (10% twins), ovarian hyperstimulation
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Metformin – avoid if eGFR <30; reduce dose if eGFR 30-45
  - Spironolactone – contraindicated if eGFR <30 (hyperkalaemia)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Clomifene >150 mg/day** – increases risk of multiple pregnancy + OHSS. Stick to 50 mg as starting dose. Never prescribe without baseline pelvic ultrasound.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) – with clomifene or gonadotropins (abdominal distension, pain, oliguria, haemoconcentration)
  - Breakthrough bleeding on COCP (if missed pills)
- **Chronic:**
  - Endometrial hyperplasia/carcinoma (RR 3-4)
  - Type 2 diabetes (50% develop IGT or T2DM by age 40)
  - Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD)
  - Dyslipidaemia, hypertension, cardiovascular disease
  - Obstructive sleep apnoea
  - Infertility (anovulatory)
sections.Prognosis: - PCOS is a chronic condition; with lifestyle modification and metformin, ovulation and metabolic parameters improve. Without treatment, risk of endometrial cancer and T2DM is significant. Fertility can be restored with weight loss or clomifene. Life expectancy may be slightly reduced due to cardiovascular/metabolic burden.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check HbA1c and lipids** in every new PCOS patient – you may be the first doctor to screen for metabolic syndrome.
- **Prescribe COCP with 30 µg ethinylestradiol** (not 20 µg) for adequate cycle control and anti-androgen effect.
- **Refer to dietician** – a 5% weight loss can restore ovulation in overweight women.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t start spironolactone without COCP** – it causes foetal feminisation and is teratogenic.
- **Don’t ignore irregular bleeding** – always do TVUS/endometrial biopsy in PCOS patients >35 with persistent breakthrough bleeding.
- **Don’t diagnose PCOS on ultrasound alone** – must meet Rotterdam criteria; many women have PCOM without symptoms.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Severe abdominal pain (especially unilateral) – possible torsion or OHSS
  - Heavy vaginal bleeding >7 days or clots – possible endometrial pathology
  - Symptoms of hyperglycaemia (polydipsia, polyuria, weight loss) – undiagnosed T2DM
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP for annual HbA1c, lipids, BP
  - Gynaecology clinic if trying to conceive or requiring complex management
  - Dermatology for hirsutism if COCP insufficient
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L
  - GCS <15
  - OHSS with haemoconcentration (Hct >45%), oliguria, or respiratory distress – call gynaecology reg + ITU
  - Suspected ovarian torsion – urgent gynaecology reg review + arrange TVUS
  - New onset seizure (if on clomifene/metformin – rare, check electrolyte/glucose)

rawMarkdown:
## Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)

**One-liner:** A common endocrine disorder of reproductive-age women, characterised by hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, and polycystic ovaries; it increases long-term metabolic and cardiovascular risk.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ovarian torsion** (rare but surgical emergency): sudden, severe unilateral pelvic pain with nausea/vomiting; absent arterial flow on pelvic ultrasound is the killer finding – do not wait for lab results.
- **Endometrial adenocarcinoma** (driven by unopposed oestrogen in anovulatory cycles): postmenopausal bleeding or atypical endometrial cells on biopsy – not a first-hour emergency, but must not be missed as a chronic complication.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Oligomenorrhoea/amenorrhoea (<9 menses/year)
  - Hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey score ≥8)
  - Acne, androgenetic alopecia
  - Infertility
  - Weight gain/central obesity (BMI >30 in ~50%)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Acanthosis nigricans (insulin resistance)
  - Palpable ovarian masses? (unlikely, but consider US)
  - Blood pressure (elevated in metabolic syndrome)
  - Waist circumference >80 cm

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators**
  1. PCOS (most common) – chronic anovulation + hyperandrogenism + PCOM on US
  2. Non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (NCCAH) – elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone (>30 nmol/L)
  3. Cushing’s syndrome – features of cortisol excess (moon face, striae, proximal myopathy)
  4. Androgen-secreting tumour – rapid onset, severe virilisation, testosterone >5 nmol/L
  5. Hypothyroidism – elevated TSH, galactorrhoea (if prolactin elevated)
- **Splitter:** **Testosterone >5 nmol/L** – suggests androgen-secreting tumour (not PCOS). **17-OHP >30 nmol/L** – suggests NCCAH.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine pregnancy test (always in reproductive-age women with amenorrhoea)
  - Blood glucose (finger-prick if symptomatic)
- **Bloods:**
  - **Hormones:** total testosterone, SHBG, free androgen index (FAI), LH, FSH, prolactin, 17-OHP (cycle day 2-5 if possible, or random)
  - **Metabolic:** fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipid profile (HDL, LDL, triglycerides)
  - **Other:** TSH, serum cortisol (if Cushing’s suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transvaginal pelvic ultrasound (TVUS) – count antral follicles (≥20 in one ovary) or ovarian volume ≥10 mL
  - **Second-line:** Adrenal CT if tumour suspected (testosterone >5 nmol/L)
- **Special tests:**
  - Glucose tolerance test (75 g OGTT) if BMI >30 or metabolic syndrome
  - Endometrial biopsy if postmenopausal bleeding or atypical bleeding >6 months
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Rotterdam criteria (2003)** – requires ≥2 of:
  - Oligo‑ or anovulation
  - Clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism
  - Polycystic ovaries on US (≥20 follicles per ovary or volume ≥10 mL)
  - Exclude other causes (NCCAH, Cushing’s, tumour, thyroid)
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** – a woman with amenorrhoea could be pregnant, not PCOS. Also, **endometrial biopsy** in any PCOS patient with persistent intermenstrual bleeding or age >35 (risk of endometrial cancer).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute pelvic pain + suspected torsion: urgent gynaecology referral + ultrasound
  - If bleeding + anaemia: stabilise, arrange TVUS, consider tranexamic acid (if no VTE risk)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Not usually admitted for PCOS alone. If admitted for another reason (e.g. DKA, endometrial biopsy):
    - Check HbA1c, lipids, BP
    - Start lifestyle advice (diet, exercise, weight loss)
    - Refer to dietician
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **First-line:** Lifestyle modification (weight loss 5-10% improves ovulation)
  - **Pharmacotherapy:**
    - Combined oral contraceptive (COCP) for cycle control + anti-androgen effect (e.g. Yasmin® [drospirenone+ethinylestradiol])
    - Metformin: 500 mg TDS or 850-1000 mg BD (off-label but used for metabolic benefit)
    - Anti-androgens: spironolactone (100-200 mg/day) – only with COCP (contraception required, teratogenic)
    - Infertility: clomifene citrate (50-150 mg on days 2-6) – **IAN** endocrine/specialist supervision
  - **Endometrial protection:** Progesterone 10 mg for 14 days per month if unopposed oestrogen
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Not specific for PCOS; use standard hospital policy for surgery/observation

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **COCP:** generic ethinylestradiol 30 µg + levonorgestrel 150 µg daily for 21 days, 7-day break
  - **Metformin:** start 500 mg daily, increase to 500 mg TDS or 850-1000 mg BD (max 2000 mg/day)
  - **Spironolactone:** 100-200 mg daily (only with COCP)
  - **Clomifene citrate:** 50 mg daily ×5 days (cycle days 2-6); max 150 mg daily if anovulatory
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Cyproterone acetate (anti-androgen) – 25-100 mg daily ×10 days/month (use as last-line due to hepatotoxicity)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - COCP – contraindicated in BMI >35 (VTE risk), migraine with aura, uncontrolled hypertension, smoking >35 years
  - Metformin – hold if eGFR <30, risk of lactic acidosis (rare)
  - Clomifene – risk of multiple gestation (10% twins), ovarian hyperstimulation
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Metformin – avoid if eGFR <30; reduce dose if eGFR 30-45
  - Spironolactone – contraindicated if eGFR <30 (hyperkalaemia)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Clomifene >150 mg/day** – increases risk of multiple pregnancy + OHSS. Stick to 50 mg as starting dose. Never prescribe without baseline pelvic ultrasound.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) – with clomifene or gonadotropins (abdominal distension, pain, oliguria, haemoconcentration)
  - Breakthrough bleeding on COCP (if missed pills)
- **Chronic:**
  - Endometrial hyperplasia/carcinoma (RR 3-4)
  - Type 2 diabetes (50% develop IGT or T2DM by age 40)
  - Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD)
  - Dyslipidaemia, hypertension, cardiovascular disease
  - Obstructive sleep apnoea
  - Infertility (anovulatory)

### Prognosis
- PCOS is a chronic condition; with lifestyle modification and metformin, ovulation and metabolic parameters improve. Without treatment, risk of endometrial cancer and T2DM is significant. Fertility can be restored with weight loss or clomifene. Life expectancy may be slightly reduced due to cardiovascular/metabolic burden.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check HbA1c and lipids** in every new PCOS patient – you may be the first doctor to screen for metabolic syndrome.
- **Prescribe COCP with 30 µg ethinylestradiol** (not 20 µg) for adequate cycle control and anti-androgen effect.
- **Refer to dietician** – a 5% weight loss can restore ovulation in overweight women.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t start spironolactone without COCP** – it causes foetal feminisation and is teratogenic.
- **Don’t ignore irregular bleeding** – always do TVUS/endometrial biopsy in PCOS patients >35 with persistent breakthrough bleeding.
- **Don’t diagnose PCOS on ultrasound alone** – must meet Rotterdam criteria; many women have PCOM without symptoms.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Severe abdominal pain (especially unilateral) – possible torsion or OHSS
  - Heavy vaginal bleeding >7 days or clots – possible endometrial pathology
  - Symptoms of hyperglycaemia (polydipsia, polyuria, weight loss) – undiagnosed T2DM
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP for annual HbA1c, lipids, BP
  - Gynaecology clinic if trying to conceive or requiring complex management
  - Dermatology for hirsutism if COCP insufficient

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L
  - GCS <15
  - OHSS with haemoconcentration (Hct >45%), oliguria, or respiratory distress – call gynaecology reg + ITU
  - Suspected ovarian torsion – urgent gynaecology reg review + arrange TVUS
  - New onset seizure (if on clomifene/metformin – rare, check electrolyte/glucose)
